one_line_takeaway: A GRPO-trained 1.5B policy with a learned pairwise-comparison reward turns automated prompt injection into a solvable dense-reward RL problem, beating template attacks, GCG/TAP/evolutionary-search baselines, and even a purpose-built defense (Meta-SecAlign-70B, 21.88% ASR vs. ~0% for all templates) while often preserving or exceeding baseline task utility.
key_finding: AutoInject substantially outperforms template attacks on production models: 58.00% ASR vs. a peak template baseline of 23.60% (Important Instructions) on Gemini-2.5-flash; 47.97% vs. 20.48% (Tool Knowledge) on GPT-4.1-nano; 11.49% vs. 5.69% (Tool Knowledge) on GPT-5-nano; and 12.59% vs. 5.69%-scale peaks on Claude-3.5-Sonnet (accessed via OpenRouter with Bedrock Guardrails). Notably, utility under attack often exceeds the no-attack baseline utility (e.g. 90.20% vs. 80.41% on GPT-5-nano, 98.52% vs. 75.00% on Claude-3.5-Sonnet), because utility is part of the optimized reward. Against optimization-based baselines on Qwen3-4B/Gemma3-4B, AutoInject reaches 42.50%/35.00% ASR vs. GCG's 23.00%/20.20%, TAP's 36.60%/—, and evolutionary search's 40.00%/35.00% (competitive on these smaller open-weight targets); on a harder GPT-5-nano banking-suite comparison AutoInject reaches 36.57% ASR vs. evolutionary search's 13.9%. Against Meta-SecAlign-70B, a model whose SecAlign++ training drives all six template attacks to ~0% ASR, AutoInject still achieves 21.88% ASR while keeping utility (39.29%) above the no-attack baseline (37.50%). In transfer experiments against RL-Hammer (trained with 114 tasks / 3,900-query budget vs. AutoInject's 5 tasks / 260 queries), AutoInject matches or exceeds RL-Hammer on 4/5 targets in fixed-injection transfer (e.g. 45.0% vs. 22.9% ASR on GPT-4o-mini, p<0.05) and significantly beats it on 4/5 targets in cross-task transfer (e.g. 55.8% vs. 0.0% ASR on Gemini-2.5-flash), losing only on GPT-5-nano where RL-Hammer's training distribution directly overlaps two of the eight held-out task pairs. Ablations show both GRPO training and the learned feedback model matter most on harder targets: removing GRPO drops GPT-5-nano ASR from 36.57% to 19.44% (47% relative drop) vs. only 97.69%→88.32% on GPT-4.1-nano (10% relative drop); removing the feedback model drops GPT-5-nano ASR from 36.57% to 25.00% (11.57 points) vs. a marginal 2.5-point drop on GPT-4.1-nano.
core_contribution: AutoInject, a black-box reinforcement learning framework (GRPO over a 1.5B-parameter Qwen2.5 policy) that learns adversarial suffixes for prompt injection attacks against LLM agents. Its central methodological contribution is converting the binary (mostly zero) prompt-injection success signal into a dense reward by training a learned comparison-based feedback model: each candidate suffix is scored against the best suffix seen so far via a chain-of-thought preference judgment (rpref = P(x ≻ x* | g, c)), and this is combined with the ground-truth security score (rsec) and an optional utility score (rutil) into a composite reward R = alpha*rsec + beta*rutil + gamma*rpref that is optimized with GRPO. The framework supports both online query-based attacks against a live victim (with utility access) and offline-trained transferable suffixes that require no utility signal at deployment.
threat_model: Black-box attacker with only query access to a victim LLM-agent pipeline (AgentDojo-style: user task + externally injected content, e.g. an email body, processed by the agent). The attacker can observe a binary security outcome (whether the injected goal's tool call executed) in any setting; access to a utility signal (whether the legitimate user task also completed) is treated as a stronger assumption realistic for attacker-controlled/offline optimization or defender-side red-teaming, and is dropped entirely in the transfer setting where suffixes are optimized offline on surrogate models/tasks and deployed with no live signal. The attacker does not require token log-probabilities of the victim model (unlike GCG or the concurrent Panfilov et al. 2026 method) and does not require injecting an auxiliary model into the target environment (unlike RL-Hammer).
limitations: Utility-signal access is a materially stronger assumption than security-signal access, and the paper is explicit that it only holds in attacker-controlled/offline or defender-side settings, not general live deployment. AgentDojo's evaluation pipeline assumes the victim model executes tool calls immediately upon receiving instructions; the authors observe that models exhibiting deliberation behavior (e.g., Claude Haiku 4.5 requesting clarification before acting) break this pipeline assumption, suggesting a potential defense the paper does not evaluate against. The threat model also assumes the target's tool-calling decisions can be influenced by processed content, which plan-then-execute architectures (CaMeL, IPI-Guard) are designed to structurally prevent; the authors acknowledge these are out of scope and note they trade away agent flexibility. The evolutionary-search baseline (Nasr et al. 2025) is a reimplementation, not the original released code, since the original authors did not release it. Claude-3.5-Sonnet is evaluated only on a 144-task banking subset (not the full 949-pair AgentDojo benchmark) due to API cost, and is accessed through OpenRouter with an added Bedrock Guardrails defense layer, confounding the reported low ASR with that extra filtering layer. Meta-SecAlign-70B evaluation uses only the 8 hardest task pairs per suite, not the full benchmark. The paper is a preprint (arXiv 2602.05746v2) and has not undergone peer review at time of this text extraction.

datasets_used: AgentDojo (Debenedetti et al., 2024): 97 user tasks across banking, travel, workspace, and Slack domains, each paired with multiple injection tasks; full benchmark = 949 (user task, injection task) pairs
144-task banking suite subset of AgentDojo (used for Claude-3.5-Sonnet due to API cost)
20-pair-per-suite task selection (5 user tasks x 4 injection tasks) matching Hofer et al. (2025) for baseline comparisons on Qwen3-4B/Gemma3-4B
8 hardest user tasks x 4 hardest injection tasks per suite for Meta-SecAlign-70B evaluation
16 banking user tasks with a fixed injection task (fixed-injection transfer) and 8 cross-suite (user task, injection task) pairs (cross-task transfer)
quantitative_results: ASR on Gemini-2.5-flash: AutoInject 58.00% vs. best template (Important Instructions) 23.60%
ASR on GPT-4.1-nano: AutoInject 47.97% vs. best template (Tool Knowledge) 20.48%
ASR on GPT-5-nano: AutoInject 11.49% vs. best template (Tool Knowledge) 5.69%
ASR on Claude-3.5-Sonnet (banking subset, via OpenRouter/Bedrock Guardrails): AutoInject 12.59% vs. best template (Tool Knowledge) 5.69%
Utility under attack exceeding no-attack baseline: GPT-5-nano 90.20% vs. 80.41% baseline; Claude-3.5-Sonnet 98.52% vs. 75.00% baseline
Meta-SecAlign-70B: AutoInject 21.88% ASR / 39.29% utility vs. all six template attacks at 0.00%-3.12% ASR (no-attack utility baseline 37.50%)
Qwen3-4B ASR: AutoInject 42.50%, Evolutionary Search 40.00%, TAP 36.60%, Adaptive Attack 30.00%, GCG 23.00%, Direct Instruction 11.20%, Random Prefix-Suffix 9.70%
Gemma3-4B ASR: AutoInject 35.00%, Evolutionary Search 35.00%, Adaptive Attack 26.25%, GCG 20.20%, Direct Instruction 6.70%, Random Prefix-Suffix 5.90%
Full banking suite, GPT-5-nano target: AutoInject 36.57% ASR vs. Evolutionary Search 13.9% ASR
Fixed-injection transfer vs. RL-Hammer (GPT-4o-mini target): AutoInject 45.0%±9.2% ASR vs. RL-Hammer 22.9%±2.9% ASR (p<0.05)
Cross-task transfer vs. RL-Hammer (Gemini-2.5-flash target): AutoInject 55.8%±14.1% ASR vs. RL-Hammer 0.0%±0.0% ASR; RL-Hammer wins only on GPT-5-nano (25.0% vs. AutoInject's 10.0%)

Ablation on GPT-5-nano (banking suite): full AutoInject 36.57% ASR / 87.50% utility; w/o GRPO 19.44% ASR / 87.50% utility; w/o feedback model 25.00% ASR / 84.03% utility; w/o both 17.36% ASR / 87.50% utility

Ablation on GPT-4.1-nano (banking suite): full AutoInject 97.69% ASR / 62.50% utility; w/o GRPO 88.32% ASR / 51.82% utility; w/o feedback model 95.14% ASR / 59.02% utility; w/o both 90.51% ASR / 50.36% utility
Recurring 'allelujah' token pattern in transferred suffixes compromises up to 70 (user task, injection task) pairs on Gemini-2.5-flash
baselines_compared: AgentDojo template attacks: Direct Instruction, Ignore Previous, Important Instructions, InjecAgent, System Message, Tool Knowledge
GCG (Zou et al., 2023) - white-box gradient suffix optimization
TAP (Tree of Attacks with Pruning; Mehrotra et al., 2024) - black-box tree search
Random adaptive attack (Andriushchenko et al., 2024) - jailbreak baseline adapted with feedback-model preferences
RL-Hammer (Wen et al., 2025) - transfer-based RL attack policy requiring an auxiliary model injected into the target environment
Evolutionary search with LLM-based mutation (reimplementation of Nasr et al., 2025; original code not released)
Search-based adaptive attack ablation baseline (same reward function/query budget as AutoInject, but random 30% mutation instead of a learned policy)
